name: Trigonometry
description: A rigorous study of trigonometric functions, identities, and their applications. Topics include the unit circle, right-triangle and circular definitions, graphing, inverse functions, trigonometric equations, the Laws of Sines and Cosines, polar coordinates, vectors, and complex numbers — providing the mathematical foundation for calculus and physics.

modules.title: The Unit Circle
modules.objectives: Define the unit circle; convert between degrees and radians; identify coordinates of standard angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°, and their multiples).
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Unit Circle Definition of Trig Functions
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Unit Circle Practice (20 problems)
modules.assignment: Memorize all standard angles and their coordinates on the unit circle. Create a labeled unit circle diagram from memory and verify 10 angle coordinates.

modules.title: Trigonometric Functions
modules.objectives: Define sin, cos, tan, csc, sec, cot using the unit circle; evaluate trig functions at standard angles; understand the domain and range of each function.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 7.2–7.3: Trig Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Trig Values of Special Angles
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Trigonometric Functions of Any Angle
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Trig Functions of Special Angles (25 problems)
modules.assignment: Evaluate all six trig functions for 8 standard angles. Write and solve 5 real-world problems involving angle and ratio relationships.

modules.title: Graphs of Trigonometric Functions
modules.objectives: Identify amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift; graph y = A sin(Bx + C) + D and y = A cos(Bx + C) + D; sketch tan and cot graphs.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 8.1–8.2: Graphs of Trig Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Amplitude of Sinusoidal Functions
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Graphing Sine and Cosine
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Graphs of Sine and Cosine (20 problems)
modules.assignment: Graph 6 sinusoidal functions labeling all key features. Write the equation of a sinusoidal function from a given graph.

modules.title: Trigonometric Identities
modules.objectives: State and prove the Pythagorean, reciprocal, and quotient identities; use identities to simplify trig expressions and verify equations.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 9.1: Verifying Trigonometric Identities
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Applying Pythagorean Identities (15 problems)
modules.assignment: Verify 8 trigonometric identities step by step. Simplify 6 complex trig expressions using fundamental identities.

modules.title: Sum & Difference Formulas
modules.objectives: Apply sum and difference identities for sine, cosine, and tangent; evaluate trig functions at non-standard angles using these formulas.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 9.2: Sum and Difference Identities
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Proof: Angle Addition for Cosine
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Sum and Difference Formulas
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Sum and Difference Identities Practice (15 problems)
modules.assignment: Use sum/difference formulas to find exact values of sin(75°), cos(15°), tan(105°), and 5 more. Verify two identities using these formulas.

modules.title: Double-Angle & Half-Angle Formulas
modules.objectives: Derive and apply double-angle formulas for sin, cos, and tan; use half-angle formulas to find exact values; simplify expressions using power-reducing formulas.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 9.3: Double-Angle, Half-Angle, and Reduction Formulas
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Double Angle Formula for Sine
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Double Angle and Half Angle Formulas
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Double Angle Identities Practice (15 problems)
modules.assignment: Use double-angle formulas to find sin(2theta), cos(2theta) given sin(theta) or cos(theta) for 6 cases. Use half-angle formulas to evaluate 4 exact values.

modules.title: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
modules.objectives: Define arcsin, arccos, and arctan with their restricted domains and ranges; evaluate inverse trig expressions; compose trig and inverse trig functions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 8.3: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Intro to Arcsin, Arccos, Arctan
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Inverse Trig Functions Practice (20 problems)
modules.assignment: Evaluate 10 inverse trig expressions exactly. Solve 5 equations using inverse trig functions and verify by substitution.

modules.title: Right Triangle Trigonometry
modules.objectives: Apply SOH-CAH-TOA to right triangles; find unknown sides and angles; solve real-world problems involving angles of elevation and depression.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 7.2: Right Triangle Trigonometry
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Right Triangle Trig Practice (25 problems)
modules.assignment: Solve 8 right triangles completely. Write and solve 4 application problems involving angles of elevation and depression.

modules.title: Law of Sines
modules.objectives: State and derive the Law of Sines; apply it to solve ASA, AAS, and SSA (ambiguous case) triangles; determine the number of solutions in the ambiguous case.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 10.1: Law of Sines
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Law of Sines and the Ambiguous Case
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Law of Sines Practice (20 problems)
modules.assignment: Solve 6 oblique triangles using Law of Sines (include 2 ambiguous SSA cases). Write 2 real-world navigation problems and solve them.

modules.title: Law of Cosines
modules.objectives: State and derive the Law of Cosines; apply it to SAS and SSS triangles; find the area of a triangle using Heron's formula.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 10.2: Law of Cosines
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Law of Cosines Practice (15 problems)
modules.assignment: Solve 6 triangles using Law of Cosines. Calculate the area of 4 triangles using Heron's formula. Write a surveying application problem.

modules.title: Polar Coordinates
modules.objectives: Convert between rectangular and polar coordinates; graph polar equations; identify common polar curves (roses, limacons, circles, spirals).
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 10.3: Polar Coordinates
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Polar Coordinates Practice (20 problems)
modules.assignment: Convert 8 points between rectangular and polar forms. Graph 4 polar equations labeling key points. Identify and classify 3 polar curves.

modules.title: Vectors
modules.objectives: Define vectors in component and magnitude-direction form; perform vector addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication; compute dot products and find angles between vectors.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Intro to Vectors and Scalars
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Vectors in the Plane
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Vector Addition Practice (20 problems)
modules.assignment: Perform 8 vector operations (addition, subtraction, scalar multiplication). Calculate dot products and angles between 4 pairs of vectors. Solve 2 force/velocity application problems.

modules.title: Complex Numbers in Trigonometric Form
modules.objectives: Write complex numbers in polar/trigonometric form; apply De Moivre's Theorem to find powers and roots of complex numbers.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 10.5: Polar Form of Complex Numbers
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Complex Numbers in Polar Form
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Complex Numbers in Polar Form and De Moivre's Theorem
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Multiply Complex Numbers in Polar Form (15 problems)
modules.assignment: Convert 6 complex numbers to polar form. Use De Moivre's Theorem to find the 4th power of 2 complex numbers and all cube roots of 2 complex numbers.

modules.title: Applications of Trigonometry
modules.objectives: Model periodic phenomena with sinusoidal functions; solve applied problems involving navigation, physics, engineering, and harmonic motion.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra & Trigonometry 2e — Ch 8.1 Applications: Modeling with Sinusoidal Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Modeling with Trig: Daily Temperature
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Applications of Trigonometric Functions
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Modeling with Trig Functions (15 problems)
modules.assignment: Build sinusoidal models for 3 real-world datasets (tides, temperature, sound). Solve 3 navigation problems using bearings and trig. Write a 1-page reflection on where trig appears in your daily life.

quizQuestions.question: How many radians are in 180 degrees?
quizQuestions.explanation: 180 degrees = pi radians is the fundamental degree-radian conversion.

quizQuestions.question: What are the coordinates of the point at 60 degrees on the unit circle?
quizQuestions.explanation: At 60 degrees, x = cos(60) = 1/2 and y = sin(60) = sqrt(3)/2.

quizQuestions.question: Convert 270 degrees to radians.
What is the radius of the unit circle?
quizQuestions.explanation: By definition, the unit circle has radius 1 centered at the origin.

quizQuestions.question: What are the coordinates at 150 degrees on the unit circle?
quizQuestions.explanation: 150 degrees is in Quadrant II. cos(150) = -sqrt(3)/2 and sin(150) = 1/2.

quizQuestions.question: What is sin(pi/6)?
quizQuestions.explanation: sin(30 degrees) = sin(pi/6) = 1/2 from the unit circle.

quizQuestions.question: What is the domain of f(x) = tan(x)?
options: x not equal to pi/2 + n*pi
quizQuestions.answer: x not equal to pi/2 + n*pi
quizQuestions.explanation: tan(x) = sin(x)/cos(x) is undefined where cos(x) = 0, i.e., at x = pi/2 + n*pi.

quizQuestions.question: What is cos(pi)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The point at angle pi (180 degrees) on the unit circle is (-1, 0), so cos(pi) = -1.

quizQuestions.question: What is the range of the sine function?
quizQuestions.explanation: The y-coordinates on the unit circle range from -1 to 1, so sin(x) is in [-1, 1].

quizQuestions.question: If sin(theta) = 3/5 and theta is in Quadrant II, what is cos(theta)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Using sin^2 + cos^2 = 1: cos^2 = 1 - 9/25 = 16/25, so cos = +/-4/5. In QII, cosine is negative, so cos(theta) = -4/5.

quizQuestions.question: What is the period of y = sin(2x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The period of y = sin(Bx) is 2pi/B = 2pi/2 = pi.

quizQuestions.question: What is the amplitude of y = -3cos(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Amplitude is |A| = |-3| = 3. Amplitude is always positive.

quizQuestions.question: What is the phase shift of y = sin(x - pi/4)?
quizQuestions.explanation: y = sin(x - C) shifts the graph C units to the right. Here C = pi/4.

quizQuestions.question: What is the vertical shift of y = cos(x) + 3?
quizQuestions.explanation: The +3 at the end shifts the entire graph 3 units upward.

quizQuestions.question: What is the period of y = tan(pi*x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The period of y = tan(Bx) is pi/B = pi/pi = 1.

quizQuestions.question: Which identity states that sin^2(theta) + cos^2(theta) = 1?
quizQuestions.explanation: sin^2(theta) + cos^2(theta) = 1 is derived from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the unit circle.

quizQuestions.question: Simplify: (1 - cos^2(theta)) / sin(theta)
quizQuestions.explanation: 1 - cos^2(theta) = sin^2(theta), so the expression becomes sin^2/sin = sin(theta).

quizQuestions.question: What is tan(theta) in terms of sin and cos?
quizQuestions.explanation: By the quotient identity, tan(theta) = sin(theta) / cos(theta).

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following equals 1 + tan^2(theta)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Dividing sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 by cos^2 gives tan^2 + 1 = sec^2.

quizQuestions.question: Simplify: sec(theta) * sin(theta)
quizQuestions.explanation: sec(theta) * sin(theta) = (1/cos) * sin = sin/cos = tan(theta).

quizQuestions.question: What is sin(A + B)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The sum formula for sine is sin(A + B) = sinA cosB + cosA sinB.

quizQuestions.question: Using the sum formula, what is cos(90 degrees)?
What is cos(A - B)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The difference formula for cosine is cos(A - B) = cosA cosB + sinA sinB.

quizQuestions.question: Find the exact value of sin(75 degrees).
What is tan(A + B)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The sum formula for tangent is tan(A + B) = (tanA + tanB)/(1 - tanA tanB).

quizQuestions.question: What is sin(2theta) in terms of sin and cos?
quizQuestions.explanation: The double-angle formula: sin(2theta) = 2*sin(theta)*cos(theta).

quizQuestions.question: Which is NOT a valid form of cos(2theta)?
quizQuestions.explanation: 2*sin*cos = sin(2theta), not cos(2theta). The three valid forms are cos^2-sin^2, 2cos^2-1, and 1-2sin^2.

quizQuestions.question: If sin(theta) = 3/5 and theta is in QI, what is sin(2theta)?
What is sin(theta/2) in terms of cos(theta)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The half-angle formula: sin(theta/2) = +/- sqrt((1 - cos(theta))/2).

quizQuestions.question: Use the double-angle formula to find cos(60) from cos(30) = sqrt(3)/2.
What is the range of arcsin(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The range of arcsin is restricted to [-pi/2, pi/2] to ensure it is a function.

quizQuestions.question: Evaluate arccos(-1).
quizQuestions.explanation: arccos(-1) = pi because cos(pi) = -1 and pi is in the range [0, pi] of arccos.

quizQuestions.question: What is the domain of arcsin(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: arcsin is the inverse of sin, so its domain equals the range of sin, which is [-1, 1].

quizQuestions.question: Evaluate arctan(1).
quizQuestions.explanation: arctan(1) = pi/4 because tan(pi/4) = 1 and pi/4 is in the range (-pi/2, pi/2) of arctan.

quizQuestions.question: Simplify sin(arccos(x)).
quizQuestions.explanation: Let theta = arccos(x), so cos(theta) = x. By the Pythagorean identity, sin(theta) = sqrt(1 - x^2) (positive since arccos gives angles in [0, pi]).

quizQuestions.question: In a right triangle, what does SOH stand for?
quizQuestions.explanation: SOH: Sine = Opposite/Hypotenuse. CAH: Cosine = Adjacent/Hypotenuse. TOA: Tangent = Opposite/Adjacent.

quizQuestions.question: A 10-meter ladder leans against a wall at 60 degrees with the ground. How high does it reach?
quizQuestions.explanation: Height = 10 * sin(60) = 10 * (sqrt(3)/2) = 5*sqrt(3) meters.

quizQuestions.question: In a right triangle with hypotenuse 13 and one leg 5, what is the sine of the angle opposite the 5-unit leg?
quizQuestions.explanation: sin(theta) = opposite/hypotenuse = 5/13.

quizQuestions.question: What is the angle of elevation to the top of a 100 m tower from a point 100 m away?
quizQuestions.explanation: tan(theta) = 100/100 = 1, so theta = arctan(1) = 45 degrees.

quizQuestions.question: If cos(theta) = 7/25, find sin(theta) in a right triangle.
State the Law of Sines.
quizQuestions.explanation: The Law of Sines states that the ratio of each side to the sine of its opposite angle is constant.

quizQuestions.question: In triangle ABC, A = 40 degrees, B = 75 degrees, a = 10. Find b (approximately).
The ambiguous case (SSA) can have how many possible triangles?
quizQuestions.explanation: In the SSA configuration, there may be 0, 1, or 2 valid triangles — this is the ambiguous case.

quizQuestions.question: In triangle ABC, A = 30 degrees, a = 6, b = 12. How many triangles are possible?
quizQuestions.explanation: Height h = b*sinA = 12*sin(30) = 6 = a. When a equals the height, exactly one right triangle is formed.

quizQuestions.question: Which case can be solved directly using Law of Sines without ambiguity?
quizQuestions.explanation: AAS (two angles and a non-included side) has exactly one solution and is solved directly by the Law of Sines.

quizQuestions.question: State the Law of Cosines for side a.
quizQuestions.explanation: The Law of Cosines: a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc*cosA, relating all three sides and one angle.

quizQuestions.question: In triangle ABC, a = 8, b = 6, C = 90 degrees. Find c.
quizQuestions.explanation: c^2 = 64 + 36 - 2*8*6*cos(90) = 100 - 0 = 100, so c = 10. This is the Pythagorean theorem.

quizQuestions.question: In Heron's formula, what is s?
quizQuestions.explanation: s is the semi-perimeter: s = (a + b + c)/2. Then Area = sqrt(s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)).

quizQuestions.question: When should you use the Law of Cosines instead of the Law of Sines?
options: When you know SAS or SSS
quizQuestions.answer: When you know SAS or SSS
quizQuestions.explanation: The Law of Cosines is used for SAS (two sides and included angle) and SSS (all three sides).

quizQuestions.question: In a triangle with a = 5, b = 7, c = 8, what is cos(C)?
The polar point (3, pi/2) corresponds to which rectangular point?
quizQuestions.explanation: x = 3*cos(pi/2) = 0; y = 3*sin(pi/2) = 3. So the point is (0, 3).

quizQuestions.question: Convert the rectangular point (-3, 3) to polar form.
quizQuestions.explanation: r = sqrt(9+9) = 3*sqrt(2). The point is in QII so theta = 3pi/4.

quizQuestions.question: What shape does r = 4 represent in polar coordinates?
options: A circle of radius 4 centered at the origin
quizQuestions.answer: A circle of radius 4 centered at the origin
quizQuestions.explanation: r = constant is a circle centered at the pole with the given radius.

quizQuestions.question: How many petals does the rose curve r = cos(3*theta) have?
quizQuestions.explanation: For r = cos(n*theta) with n odd, the curve has n petals. Here n = 3, so 3 petals.

quizQuestions.question: Which polar equation represents a limacon?
quizQuestions.explanation: A limacon has the form r = a +/- b*cos(theta) or r = a +/- b*sin(theta). Here r = 1 + 2cos(theta) fits this form.

quizQuestions.question: What is the magnitude of vector v = <3, 4>?
What is the dot product of <2, 3> and <4, -1>?
Two vectors are perpendicular when their dot product is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Two vectors are perpendicular (orthogonal) if and only if their dot product equals 0.

quizQuestions.question: Find the unit vector in the direction of <3, 4>.
quizQuestions.explanation: The magnitude is 5. The unit vector is <3/5, 4/5>.

quizQuestions.question: What is the angle between vectors <1, 0> and <0, 1>?
quizQuestions.explanation: cos(theta) = dot product / (|u|*|v|) = 0/(1*1) = 0, so theta = 90 degrees.

quizQuestions.question: Write 1 + i in polar form.
quizQuestions.explanation: |1+i| = sqrt(2), arg(1+i) = arctan(1) = pi/4. So 1+i = sqrt(2)*cis(pi/4).

quizQuestions.question: De Moivre's Theorem: [r*(cos(theta) + i*sin(theta))]^n = ?
quizQuestions.explanation: De Moivre's Theorem: [r*cis(theta)]^n = r^n * cis(n*theta).

quizQuestions.question: Compute (1 + i)^4 using De Moivre's Theorem.
quizQuestions.explanation: 1+i = sqrt(2)*cis(pi/4). Raised to the 4th: (sqrt(2))^4 * cis(pi) = 4*(-1) = -4.

quizQuestions.question: How many nth roots does a nonzero complex number have?
quizQuestions.explanation: Every nonzero complex number has exactly n distinct nth roots, evenly spaced around a circle.

quizQuestions.question: What is |3 - 4i|?
A Ferris wheel with diameter 40 m completes one revolution in 30 seconds. What is the amplitude of the height function?
quizQuestions.explanation: Amplitude = radius = diameter/2 = 40/2 = 20 meters.

quizQuestions.question: Which trig function best models periodic oscillating phenomena?
quizQuestions.explanation: Sinusoidal functions (sin and cos) naturally model periodic, oscillating behavior around a midline.

quizQuestions.question: A sound wave has frequency 440 Hz. What is its period in seconds?
quizQuestions.explanation: Period T = 1/frequency = 1/440 seconds.

quizQuestions.question: In navigation, a bearing of N 30 degrees E means:
quizQuestions.explanation: Navigation bearings are measured clockwise from North. N 30 E means 30 degrees toward the East from North.

quizQuestions.question: What does the amplitude of a sinusoidal model represent in context?
options: Half the distance between max and min values
quizQuestions.answer: Half the distance between max and min values
quizQuestions.explanation: Amplitude = (max - min)/2, representing how far the phenomenon swings above or below its average.

questions.question: Convert 5pi/6 radians to degrees.
What are the coordinates of the point at 5pi/4 on the unit circle?
questions.explanation: 5pi/4 is in QIII (225 degrees). cos(225) = -sqrt(2)/2 and sin(225) = -sqrt(2)/2.

questions.question: What is the period of y = 3cos(x/2)?
questions.explanation: Period = 2pi/B = 2pi/(1/2) = 4pi.

questions.question: Simplify (sin^2(theta) - 1) / cos(theta).
questions.explanation: sin^2 - 1 = -cos^2 (Pythagorean identity). So (-cos^2)/cos = -cos(theta).

questions.question: Evaluate cos(pi/12) using the difference formula cos(pi/4 - pi/6).
questions.explanation: cos(pi/4 - pi/6) = cos(pi/4)cos(pi/6) + sin(pi/4)sin(pi/6) = (sqrt(2)/2)(sqrt(3)/2) + (sqrt(2)/2)(1/2) = (sqrt(6)+sqrt(2))/4.

questions.question: If cos(theta) = -5/13 and pi/2 < theta < pi, what is tan(theta)?
What is the range of f(x) = 2sin(x) + 1?
questions.explanation: 2sin(x) has range [-2, 2], so adding 1 shifts it to [-1, 3].

questions.question: Evaluate arcsin(-sqrt(3)/2).
questions.explanation: arcsin(-sqrt(3)/2) = -pi/3 because sin(-pi/3) = -sqrt(3)/2 and -pi/3 is in [-pi/2, pi/2].

questions.question: A right triangle has legs of 7 and 24. What is the sine of the angle opposite the leg of length 7?
questions.explanation: Hypotenuse = sqrt(49 + 576) = sqrt(625) = 25. sin(theta) = 7/25.

questions.question: Which expression equals csc^2(theta) - cot^2(theta)?
questions.explanation: From 1 + cot^2 = csc^2, we get csc^2 - cot^2 = 1.

questions.question: What is cos(2theta) if sin(theta) = 5/13?
In triangle ABC, A = 35 degrees, B = 65 degrees, a = 12. Find b using Law of Sines.
What is the x-intercept nearest to the origin of y = sin(x)?
questions.explanation: sin(0) = 0, so x = 0 is the x-intercept at the origin.

questions.question: Which identities are used to prove sin(A+B) = sinA*cosB + cosA*sinB?
options: Geometric/unit circle reasoning and Pythagorean identity
questions.answer: Geometric/unit circle reasoning and Pythagorean identity
questions.explanation: The sum formula is proved using the unit circle, distance formula, and the cosine difference formula.

questions.question: What is the maximum value of f(x) = -4sin(x) + 2?
questions.explanation: The maximum of -4sin(x) is 4 (when sin(x) = -1), so the maximum of -4sin(x)+2 = 4+2 = 6.

questions.question: In a triangle with sides a = 7, b = 8, c = 9, find the largest angle C.
Convert the rectangular point (0, -5) to polar form.
questions.explanation: r = sqrt(0+25) = 5. The point (0,-5) is on the negative y-axis, so theta = 3pi/2.

questions.question: Compute (sqrt(3) + i)^3 using De Moivre's Theorem.
questions.explanation: sqrt(3)+i = 2*cis(pi/6). Cubed: 2^3 * cis(3*pi/6) = 8*cis(pi/2) = 8i.

questions.question: A tide model: h(t) = 4sin(pi*t/6) + 5, where h is in feet and t in hours. What is the maximum water height?
questions.explanation: Maximum = amplitude + midline = 4 + 5 = 9 feet.

questions.question: What is the angle between vectors <1, 1> and <1, -1>?
questions.explanation: Dot product = 1*1 + 1*(-1) = 0, so the vectors are perpendicular (90 degrees).

questions.question: Solve 2sin(x) - 1 = 0 for x in [0, 2pi).
questions.explanation: sin(x) = 1/2. Solutions in [0, 2pi) are x = pi/6 and x = 5pi/6.

questions.question: What is the polar equation of a circle passing through the origin with center at (0, 2)?
questions.explanation: A circle centered at (0, a) with radius a has polar equation r = 2a*sin(theta). Here a=2, so r = 4sin(theta).

questions.question: Two forces of 30 N and 40 N act at right angles. What is the magnitude of the resultant force?
Which equals sin(3theta) in simplified form?
questions.explanation: Using sin(3theta) = sin(2theta + theta) and double-angle formulas gives 3sin - 4sin^3.

questions.question: In the ambiguous SSA case with A = 40 degrees, a = 10, b = 15, how many triangles exist?
questions.explanation: Height h = b*sinA = 15*sin(40) = 9.64. Since h < a < b (9.64 < 10 < 15), there are 2 triangles.

questions.question: What is arctan(-1)?
questions.explanation: arctan(-1) = -pi/4 because tan(-pi/4) = -1 and -pi/4 is in (-pi/2, pi/2).

questions.question: To prove sec(theta) - cos(theta) = sin(theta)*tan(theta), what is the key identity used?
questions.explanation: sec - cos = 1/cos - cos = (1-cos^2)/cos = sin^2/cos = sin*(sin/cos) = sin*tan. Key: 1-cos^2 = sin^2.

questions.question: Find the area of a triangle with sides 6 and 8 and included angle 30 degrees.
A 50-foot building casts a 50-foot shadow. What is the angle of elevation of the sun?
questions.explanation: tan(theta) = 50/50 = 1, so theta = arctan(1) = 45 degrees.

questions.question: What is the projection of <4, 3> onto <1, 0>?
questions.explanation: Projection of v onto u = (v dot u / |u|^2)*u = (4/1)*<1,0> = <4, 0>.

questions.question: The graph of y = cos(x) is the same as y = sin(x) shifted by how much?
questions.explanation: cos(x) = sin(x + pi/2), so the cosine graph is the sine graph shifted pi/2 to the left.

questions.question: Find all cube roots of 8 in polar form.
questions.explanation: 8 = 8*cis(0). Cube roots: 2*cis(0 + 2pi*k/3) for k=0,1,2 gives 2, 2*cis(2pi/3), 2*cis(4pi/3).

questions.question: Using the half-angle formula, find sin(22.5 degrees).
A rose curve r = sin(4*theta) has how many petals?
questions.explanation: For r = sin(n*theta) with n even, the curve has 2n petals. Since n=4, there are 2*4=8 petals.
